title: More time to think
author: Freek Van der Herten
summary: Matthias writes that AI has shifted more of software development from typing to thinking, reviewing, and iterating. Nice reflection on how agentic coding, parallel worktrees, and voice dictation can slow individual features down while still increasing overall output. Read more

title: Logging is here!
author: Freek Van der Herten
summary: Flare now supports log collection for Laravel and PHP apps, with real-time filtering and search in the same polished interface. A nice overview of what logging adds and how to get started with the new SDK release. Read more

title: Evals in Laravel: How to Prove Your AI Output Is Actually Good
author: Freek Van der Herten
summary: Your Agent::fake() tests prove your Laravel AI feature runs — not that its output is any good. This evals a real ticket classifier with the AI SDK: a golden dataset for the fields you can check, an LLM-as-judge for the free text you can't, and a regression gate that catches a bad prompt before your customers do. Read more

title: Flare ❤️ Livewire
author: Freek Van der Herten
summary: We built deep Livewire support into Flare, making component hierarchies, lifecycle phases, method calls, and related queries visible inside traces. It looks like a solid step forward for understanding where Livewire apps spend time and where things go wrong. Read more

title: Svelte support for Flare is here!
author: Freek Van der Herten
summary: We shipped a Svelte 5 integration for Flare with an error boundary, component hierarchy reporting, and lifecycle-aware context. Looks especially useful for seeing which component broke, and where the error came from. Read more

title: New Webpack and Next.js plugins for Flare
author: Freek Van der Herten
summary: We shipped dedicated webpack and Next.js plugins for Flare that upload sourcemaps after each production build. Nice update, especially the Next.js wrapper that handles source map generation and cleanup for you. Read more

title: The Reason I Love Tempest for APIs
author: Freek Van der Herten
summary: Steve King explains why Tempest feels so nice for API work: typed request objects, declarative validation, route discovery, and a low-ceremony action flow. It is a good look at how the framework removes boilerplate without giving up clarity. Read more

title: Forgot to Update Livewire. Got Hacked
author: Freek Van der Herten
summary: Daniel Petrica tells the story of how an unpatched Livewire vulnerability on a forgotten side project exposed Mailcoach API keys and led to 50,000 spam emails being sent. It is a useful reminder to keep dormant apps updated, and a good real-world example of how Docker can limit the blast radius when something goes wrong. Read more

title: Using property hooks in PHP
author: Freek Van der Herten
summary: Michael Dyrynda shows how PHP 8.4 property hooks can replace simple computed getter methods with virtual properties. He makes the case for using them when you want a clean, property-based API for derived values. Read more

title: Validating distinct data in requests
author: Freek Van der Herten
summary: Michael Dyrynda shows how Laravel's distinct validation rule can protect nested request payloads from duplicate reference values before they corrupt relationship mapping. He also highlights the strict and ignore_case options for cases where loose comparison is not enough. Read more

title: Compound Engineering: How Every Codes With Agents
author: Freek Van der Herten
summary: Every shares how its team uses AI agents in a compounding loop of planning, working, assessing, and feeding lessons back into the system. The big idea: each feature should make the next one easier to build because the agents keep learning the codebase. Read more

title: The stack behind There There
author: Freek Van der Herten
summary: An inside look at the stack powering There There: Laravel, Inertia, React, TypeScript, Horizon, Reverb, and a bunch of Spatie packages and services. A nice overview of the pragmatic tooling choices behind the product. Read more

title: Automate your PHP security updates
author: Freek Van der Herten
summary: Yoeri shows how to automatically open a pull request when a new PHP security advisory appears. Nice little workflow that combines Laravel Health, Oh Dear, and GitHub Actions to keep apps patched quickly. Read more

title: Claude Talk Small. Code Still Big.
author: Freek Van der Herten
summary: A fun look at making coding assistants talk less, and what that actually saves in practice. The main takeaway is that shorter replies help, but most token cost still comes from the actual work: reading, reasoning, coding, and checking. Read more

title: An Update on Composer & Packagist Supply Chain Security
author: Freek Van der Herten
summary: Composer and Packagist share a solid overview of the supply chain security work already in place, what is shipping now, and what is coming next. Worth reading if you maintain PHP packages or care about how the ecosystem is hardening against package compromise. Read more

title: Locally great, globally drifting
author: Freek Van der Herten
summary: A thoughtful review of AI-generated frontend code in a real product: strong in isolated spots, but increasingly inconsistent at the system level. It also makes the case for using AI as a candidate generator and validator, not as the reviewer with the final opinion. Read more

title: Reading code with AI, not generating it
author: Freek Van der Herten
summary: A good piece on using AI to understand a large existing codebase instead of generating more code. It shares practical onboarding tactics and prompts that help map domains, dependencies, and architecture faster. Read more

title: The robots are replacing the packages
author: Freek Van der Herten
summary: An interesting take on how AI changes the build-versus-buy decision for packages. Shared problems can often be generated, while hard, external, or opinionated problems still benefit from well-maintained packages. Read more

title: The elephant in the room
author: Freek Van der Herten
summary: Josh Comeau shares a thoughtful take on AI, arguing that deep technical skill becomes more valuable, not less, as these tools improve. His point is that strong developers can use AI to amplify their work, while less experienced builders still struggle without solid architectural judgment. Read more

title: Hard truths about building in the AI era
author: Freek Van der Herten
summary: Keith Rabois shares a sharp, opinionated take on how AI is reshaping startups, careers, and product teams. The episode is especially interesting for its thoughts on hiring, the future of product management, and building with more speed and intensity. Read more

title: Prompt-Injection Guardrails in Laravel: Defend the Tools, Not the Prompt
author: Freek Van der Herten
summary: You can't out-prompt an attacker — to the model, your system instructions and a malicious support ticket are the same text. So stop defending the prompt and lock down the boundaries you actually control: tools scoped to the authenticated user server-side, middleware that screens and logs, output handled as untrusted input, a human in front of anything irreversible, and a…

title: #[RouteParameter] Does Not Bind Your Model
author: Freek Van der Herten
summary: Michael Dyrynda explains a subtle Laravel gotcha: #[RouteParameter] only reads the current route parameter value, it does not perform implicit model binding. Good reminder that the controller signature still matters when you expect a bound model inside a form request. Read more

title: Multi-Agent Orchestration in Laravel: When You Actually Need It
author: Freek Van der Herten
summary: A practical take on multi-agent setups in Laravel: another agent only earns its place when it needs its own model, tools, or instructions. Good piece on delegation tradeoffs, context handoff, and how to test routing before it becomes an expensive latency tax. Read more

title: The ghost domain problem in DNS, and what we're doing about it
author: Freek Van der Herten
summary: A good write-up on the ghost domain problem in DNS: domains removed from a registry can still appear healthy to uptime checkers because recursive resolvers keep stale delegations warm. The Oh Dear team explains the edge case and how they're tightening their resolver setup to reduce that blind spot. Read more

title: Caching get_certificate lookups in Caddy
author: Freek Van der Herten
summary: Mattias open sourced a small Caddy module that caches get_certificate HTTP lookups, avoiding a backend fetch on every TLS handshake. A nice write-up on the problem, the design, and the trade-offs. Read more

title: Rust Tutorial For PHP and JavaScript Developers
author: Freek Van der Herten
summary: Read more

title: How PHP Attributes Changed the Way I Write Livewire
author: Freek Van der Herten
summary: Bert De Swaef shows how PHP attributes made his Livewire components easier to read by attaching validation, URL sync, and event listeners directly to the properties and methods they belong to. Nice piece on how attributes reduce mental overhead, improve IDE support, and make components feel more self-documenting. Read more

title: Feature Flags in Laravel with Pennant
author: Freek Van der Herten
summary: How we use class-based Laravel Pennant features, with a kill switch on every flag and a config-driven path to general availability. Read more

title: Building RAG in Laravel: Four Ingestion Bugs That Silently Wreck Retrieval
author: Freek Van der Herten
summary: Every Laravel RAG tutorial builds the same ingestion pipeline (chunk, embed, store) and stops the moment the agent answers on screen. None of them check whether retrieval is any good. But retrieval quality is decided at ingestion, before the model runs once, and four decisions there fail with no error, no exception, no failed test: Chunking that severs the answer…

title: Architecture Decision Record
author: Freek Van der Herten
summary: A concise explanation of ADRs: short documents that capture an important decision, the context behind it, and its consequences. Good practical advice on keeping them lightweight, readable, and useful over time. Read more

title: Eloquent Query Classes Pattern
author: Freek Van der Herten
summary: Learn how to use Eloquent Query Classes to organize important database logic in Laravel without adding a full repository layer. Read more

title: Improving Laravel Architecture With Expressive
author: Freek Van der Herten
summary: Learn how Expressive can improve a Laravel application by keeping Eloquent as the database layer while moving business logic to fully typed objects. Read more

title: Context-aware headings in HTML
author: Freek Van der Herten
summary: A look at the new experimental headingoffset attribute, which lets heading levels adapt to their context instead of hardcoding h2s and h3s. A thoughtful explanation of where this could be genuinely useful, especially in component-based UIs. Read more

title: Life is too short for a slow terminal
author: Freek Van der Herten
summary: Mijndert Stuij shares a bunch of practical ways to make your terminal feel instant, from skipping shell frameworks and caching completions to lazy-loading slow tools. A good reminder that tiny bits of latency add up fast when you live in your terminal all day. Read more

title: Learn Anything With My /teach Skill
author: Freek Van der Herten
summary: Matt Pocock shows how his /teach skill turns Claude Code into a personalized teacher that adapts lessons to your goals, level, and progress. It creates structured lessons, resources, quizzes, and a learning record so you can keep building on what you learned. Read more

title: Storied Colors
author: Freek Van der Herten
summary: A lovely growing index of colors, each with its own provenance, chemistry, and often grim history. Beautifully made, and exactly the kind of rabbit hole I enjoy falling into. Read more

title: Everything About the Context Facade
author: Freek Van der Herten
summary: A deep dive into Laravel Context, from request metadata and automatic log enrichment to hidden context, scoped values, queues, scheduled commands, and internals. Read more
